questions.questionText: Down syndrome (trisomy 21) is most commonly caused by:
questions.explanation: Non-disjunction (failure of chromosome 21 to separate during meiosis I or II) is responsible for ~95% of Down syndrome cases. Robertsonian translocation accounts for ~4% (usually t(14;21)), and mosaicism accounts for ~1%. Risk increases with maternal age. Features: intellectual disability, epicanthal folds, simian crease, Brushfield spots, duodenal atresia, AV canal defect, increased risk of ALL. Reference: Thompson & Thompson — Chromosomal Disorders.

questions.questionText: Turner syndrome has the karyotype:
questions.explanation: Turner syndrome: 45,X0 (monosomy X). Features: short stature, webbed neck, shield chest, widely spaced nipples, cubitus valgus, lymphedema of hands/feet at birth, horseshoe kidney, coarctation of aorta (bicuspid aortic valve), streak gonads → primary amenorrhea, infertility. NO intellectual disability (unlike Down syndrome). Klinefelter = 47,XXY. Triple X = 47,XXX. Reference: Thompson & Thompson — Sex Chromosome Disorders.

questions.questionText: An autosomal dominant disorder with variable expressivity affecting bones, eyes, and ears is:
questions.explanation: Osteogenesis imperfecta (OI, 'brittle bone disease') is most commonly AD (COL1A1/COL1A2 mutations → abnormal type I collagen). Features: fragile bones with multiple fractures, blue sclerae, hearing loss (otosclerosis), dentinogenesis imperfecta, lax joints. Type I (mildest, most common) has blue sclerae. Type II (lethal perinatal) is the most severe. EXAM TRAP: Don't confuse OI with child abuse (non-accidental injury). Reference: Thompson & Thompson — Collagen Disorders.

questions.questionText: The inheritance pattern of hemophilia A is:
questions.explanation: Hemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency) is X-linked recessive. Males are affected, females are carriers. Affected fathers cannot pass the disease to sons (they give Y chromosome to sons). All daughters of an affected father are carriers. 50% of sons of a carrier mother will be affected. Hemophilia B (Factor IX deficiency, Christmas disease) is also X-linked recessive. Reference: Thompson & Thompson — X-Linked Inheritance.

questions.questionText: Philadelphia chromosome (t(9;22)) is associated with:
questions.explanation: Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22) results in the BCR-ABL fusion gene → constitutively active tyrosine kinase → CML. It is present in >95% of CML cases. Also found in a subset of ALL (poor prognosis). Treatment: Imatinib (tyrosine kinase inhibitor). Burkitt lymphoma = t(8;14) → c-MYC. Follicular lymphoma = t(14;18) → BCL-2. APL = t(15;17) → PML-RARα. Reference: Thompson & Thompson — Cancer Genetics.

questions.questionText: The most common autosomal recessive disorder in Caucasians is:
questions.explanation: Cystic fibrosis is the most common lethal AR disorder in Caucasians (1 in 2500 births). Caused by CFTR gene mutation (most common: ΔF508) on chromosome 7. CFTR is a chloride channel. Defect → thick, viscid secretions → recurrent lung infections (P. aeruginosa), pancreatic insufficiency, meconium ileus, male infertility. Sweat chloride test >60 mEq/L is diagnostic. Reference: Thompson & Thompson — AR Disorders.

questions.questionText: Genomic imprinting is responsible for which of the following pair of syndromes?
questions.explanation: Prader-Willi (paternal deletion 15q11-13): hypotonia, obesity, hypogonadism, intellectual disability. Angelman (maternal deletion 15q11-13): severe intellectual disability, seizures, inappropriate laughter ('happy puppet'). Same chromosomal region, different phenotype depending on parent of origin = genomic imprinting. Uniparental disomy (UPD) can also cause both. Reference: Thompson & Thompson — Epigenetics.

questions.questionText: Trinucleotide repeat expansion (CAG) is the molecular basis of:
questions.explanation: Huntington disease: CAG trinucleotide repeat expansion in the huntingtin gene (chromosome 4). It is autosomal dominant with anticipation (earlier onset and more severe in successive generations as repeats expand). Features: chorea, dementia, psychiatric symptoms (3rd-4th decade onset). Caudate nucleus atrophy. Fragile X = CGG. Myotonic dystrophy = CTG. Friedreich ataxia = GAA (AR). Reference: Thompson & Thompson — Trinucleotide Repeats.

questions.questionText: The technique used for visualizing individual chromosomes and detecting structural abnormalities is:
questions.explanation: Karyotyping involves culturing cells (usually peripheral blood lymphocytes), arresting in metaphase (using colchicine), staining (G-banding with Giemsa), and arranging chromosomes by size and banding pattern. It detects numerical abnormalities (trisomy, monosomy) and large structural abnormalities (translocations, deletions >5-10 Mb). FISH is used for specific, smaller abnormalities. PCR amplifies DNA. Reference: Thompson & Thompson — Cytogenetics.

questions.questionText: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium assumes all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium requires: (1) Large population, (2) Random mating, (3) No selection, (4) No mutation (or negligible rate), (5) No migration (gene flow). If p = frequency of dominant allele and q = frequency of recessive allele: p + q = 1, and p² + 2pq + q² = 1. Used to calculate carrier frequencies. HIGH mutation rate would disrupt equilibrium. Reference: Thompson & Thompson — Population Genetics.

questions.questionText: FISH (Fluorescence In Situ Hybridization) is used to detect:
questions.optionB: Specific chromosomal abnormalities using fluorescent probes
questions.explanation: FISH uses fluorescent-labeled DNA probes that bind to specific chromosomal regions. It can detect: microdeletions (e.g., DiGeorge 22q11, Williams 7q11), specific translocations (BCR-ABL in CML), numerical abnormalities (trisomy 21 in interphase cells), gene amplification (HER2 in breast cancer). Advantages: rapid, works on interphase cells. Cannot detect point mutations (need PCR/sequencing). Reference: Thompson & Thompson — Molecular Cytogenetics.

questions.questionText: The DNA repair mechanism defective in Xeroderma pigmentosum is:
questions.explanation: Xeroderma pigmentosum (XP): defective nucleotide excision repair (NER). NER repairs bulky DNA adducts and UV-induced thymine dimers. Patients are extremely sensitive to UV radiation → severe sunburn, freckling, skin cancers (BCC, SCC, melanoma) at young age. Autosomal recessive. Other repair defects: HNPCC (mismatch repair), Bloom/Fanconi anemia (DNA crosslink repair). Reference: Thompson & Thompson — DNA Repair.

questions.questionText: Lyon hypothesis refers to:
questions.optionB: X-inactivation (random inactivation of one X chromosome in females)
questions.explanation: Lyon hypothesis: in females (XX), one X chromosome is randomly inactivated in each cell early in embryonic development (X-inactivation), forming a Barr body. This ensures dosage compensation (similar gene expression between males and females). The inactivated X is visible as a Barr body (sex chromatin) in the nuclear membrane. Number of Barr bodies = number of X chromosomes - 1. Reference: Thompson & Thompson — X-Inactivation.

questions.questionText: Fragile X syndrome is caused by expansion of which trinucleotide repeat?
questions.explanation: Fragile X syndrome: CGG trinucleotide repeat expansion in the FMR1 gene (Xq27.3). Normal: 6-44 repeats. Premutation: 55-200 (carrier, may develop FXTAS in males). Full mutation: >200 → methylation → gene silencing. It is the most common inherited cause of intellectual disability. Features: long face, large ears, macroorchidism (postpubertal), intellectual disability, autism features. X-linked dominant with variable penetrance. Reference: Thompson & Thompson — Fragile X.

questions.questionText: The most common chromosomal abnormality in first-trimester spontaneous abortions is:
questions.explanation: Autosomal trisomies (especially trisomy 16) are the most common chromosomal abnormalities in first-trimester losses (~50% of chromosomally abnormal abortuses). Trisomy 16 is lethal and never seen in live births. Among live births, trisomy 21 is the most common autosomal trisomy. Monosomy X (Turner) accounts for ~15-20% of cytogenetically abnormal abortions. Reference: Thompson & Thompson — Chromosomal Abnormalities in Abortions.

questions.questionText: The number of Barr bodies in a patient with Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY) is:
questions.explanation: Barr bodies = number of X chromosomes - 1. Klinefelter (47,XXY): 2 X chromosomes → 1 Barr body. Normal female (46,XX): 1 Barr body. Normal male (46,XY): 0 Barr bodies. Triple X (47,XXX): 2 Barr bodies. Turner (45,X0): 0 Barr bodies. Klinefelter features: tall stature, gynecomastia, small testes, infertility, learning difficulties. Reference: Thompson & Thompson — Sex Chromatin.

questions.questionText: Marfan syndrome is caused by mutation in the gene encoding:
questions.explanation: Marfan syndrome: autosomal dominant mutation in FBN1 gene (chromosome 15) encoding fibrillin-1 (component of microfibrils in elastic fibers). Features: tall with arachnodactyly (long fingers), lens subluxation (upward), aortic root dilation (aortic regurgitation/dissection), MVP, pectus deformities, scoliosis. Ehlers-Danlos = collagen defects. OI = type I collagen defect. Homocystinuria mimics Marfan (lens subluxation downward). Reference: Thompson & Thompson — Connective Tissue Disorders.

questions.questionText: PCR (Polymerase Chain Reaction) requires all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: PCR requires: (1) DNA template, (2) Two primers (define the region to amplify), (3) Taq DNA polymerase (heat-stable, from Thermus aquaticus), (4) dNTPs (nucleotides), (5) Buffer with Mg²+. Steps: Denaturation (95°C) → Annealing (55-65°C) → Extension (72°C). RNA polymerase is NOT needed (that's for transcription). RT-PCR first converts RNA to cDNA using reverse transcriptase, then performs PCR. Reference: Thompson & Thompson — Molecular Techniques.

questions.questionText: The inheritance pattern of achondroplasia is:
questions.explanation: Achondroplasia (most common form of dwarfism) is autosomal dominant, caused by gain-of-function mutation in FGFR3 (fibroblast growth factor receptor 3) on chromosome 4. ~80% are new (de novo) mutations. Features: rhizomelic (proximal) limb shortening, normal trunk, large head with frontal bossing, trident hands, lumbar lordosis. Homozygous achondroplasia is lethal. Increased paternal age is a risk factor for new mutations. Reference: Thompson & Thompson — AD Disorders.

questions.questionText: DiGeorge syndrome is associated with microdeletion of:
questions.explanation: DiGeorge syndrome (velocardiofacial syndrome, CATCH-22): 22q11.2 microdeletion. CATCH-22: Cardiac (tetralogy of Fallot, truncus arteriosus), Abnormal facies, Thymic aplasia (T-cell immunodeficiency), Cleft palate, Hypocalcemia (hypoparathyroidism). Due to failure of 3rd and 4th pharyngeal pouch development. 15q11 = Prader-Willi/Angelman. 7q11.23 = Williams syndrome. 5p = Cri-du-chat. Reference: Thompson & Thompson — Microdeletion Syndromes.

questions.questionText: Southern blot technique is used to detect:
questions.explanation: Southern blot: detects specific DNA sequences. Northern blot: detects RNA. Western blot: detects proteins. Memory aid: 'SNoW DRoP' — Southern = DNA, Northern = RNA, Western = Protein. Southwestern blot: detects DNA-binding proteins. In Southern blot, DNA is digested with restriction enzymes, separated by gel electrophoresis, transferred to a membrane, and hybridized with labeled probes. Reference: Thompson & Thompson — Blotting Techniques.

questions.questionText: Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD) is most commonly associated with mutation in:
questions.explanation: ADPKD: PKD1 mutation (chromosome 16p13.3) accounts for ~85% of cases. PKD2 (chromosome 4) accounts for ~15%. PKD1 is more severe with earlier onset. Features: bilateral renal cysts (progressive renal failure), liver cysts, berry aneurysms (SAH risk), mitral valve prolapse. ADPKD is the most common hereditary kidney disease. ARPKD = PKHD1 gene (chromosome 6). WT1 = Wilms tumor suppressor. Reference: Thompson & Thompson — Renal Genetics.

questions.questionText: Mitochondrial inheritance is characterized by:
questions.optionB: Maternal inheritance (affected mother transmits to ALL children)
questions.explanation: Mitochondrial DNA is inherited exclusively from the mother (mitochondria in the oocyte). An affected mother passes the mutation to ALL her children (sons and daughters), but only affected females transmit further. Affected fathers do NOT transmit. Examples: MELAS (mitochondrial encephalomyopathy, lactic acidosis, stroke-like episodes), MERRF (myoclonic epilepsy with ragged red fibers), Leber hereditary optic neuropathy. Reference: Thompson & Thompson — Mitochondrial Inheritance.

questions.questionText: The enzyme used in CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing to cut DNA is:
questions.explanation: CRISPR-Cas9: a revolutionary gene editing tool. Guide RNA (gRNA) directs the Cas9 nuclease to a specific DNA sequence (complementary matching), where Cas9 makes a double-strand break. The cell's repair mechanisms then either disrupt the gene (NHEJ — non-homologous end joining) or insert a new sequence (HDR — homology-directed repair). Applications: gene therapy, disease modeling, agriculture. Reference: Thompson & Thompson — Gene Editing.

questions.questionText: Edwards syndrome (trisomy 18) is characterized by all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Edwards syndrome (trisomy 18): severe intellectual disability, low birth weight (NOT macrosomia), clenched fists (overlapping fingers — index over 3rd, 5th over 4th), rocker-bottom feet, micrognathia, prominent occiput, congenital heart defects (VSD most common), horseshoe kidney. Most die within first year. Second most common autosomal trisomy (after Down). Patau syndrome = trisomy 13 (cleft lip/palate, polydactyly, holoprosencephaly). Reference: Thompson & Thompson — Autosomal Trisomies.

questions.questionText: The tumor suppressor gene responsible for retinoblastoma is located on chromosome:
questions.explanation: RB1 gene (chromosome 13q14) is the classic tumor suppressor gene. Knudson's 'two-hit hypothesis' was based on retinoblastoma. Hereditary RB: first hit inherited (germline), second hit somatic → bilateral, earlier onset. Sporadic RB: both hits somatic → unilateral, later onset. Loss of function of BOTH alleles needed for tumor development. p53 = chromosome 17. WT1 = chromosome 11. Reference: Thompson & Thompson — Cancer Genetics.

questions.questionText: A couple has a child with sickle cell disease. What is the probability that their next child will be affected?
questions.explanation: Sickle cell disease is autosomal recessive. Both parents must be carriers (HbAS × HbAS). For each pregnancy: 25% chance of HbSS (affected), 50% chance of HbAS (carrier), 25% chance of HbAA (normal). The probability is the SAME for each pregnancy, regardless of previous outcomes (independent events). Sickle cell mutation: point mutation in beta-globin gene (GAG→GTG at codon 6, Glu→Val). Reference: Thompson & Thompson — AR Inheritance.

questions.questionText: Epigenetic modification that does NOT change the DNA sequence but alters gene expression includes:
questions.explanation: Epigenetic modifications alter gene expression WITHOUT changing the DNA sequence: (1) DNA methylation (CpG islands — methylation = gene silencing), (2) Histone modifications (acetylation → gene activation; deacetylation → silencing; methylation → context-dependent), (3) Non-coding RNA (miRNA). These are heritable but reversible. Epigenetics plays important roles in X-inactivation, imprinting, cancer. Point mutations, deletions, and translocations are genetic (DNA sequence) changes. Reference: Thompson & Thompson — Epigenetics.
